pr.title: Set up external dolphin libs from doldecomp/dolsdk2001
pr.author: ribbanya

summary: Set up an external Dolphin SDK library source tree sourced from doldecomp/dolsdk2001, intended as a work-in-progress base for gradually replacing Melee's in-tree src/dolphin files. The PR body emphasizes that each imported source file still needs manual verification and tweaking to match Melee, and that extern/dolphin/include must be manually integrated into include paths because Melee header conventions differ.
what_changed: Established or prepared an external Dolphin library area under extern/dolphin using doldecomp/dolsdk2001 as the source.
Documented that extern/dolphin is a git subtree, allowing contributions to flow between doldecomp/melee and doldecomp/dolsdk2001.
Declared the intended migration path: files currently in src/dolphin should be gradually phased out and deleted as extern/dolphin equivalents are verified for Melee.

Identified that extern/dolphin/include needs to be added to Melee include paths, but that this cannot be done mechanically because Melee's header coding conventions differ from the external SDK headers.
smart_moves: Using a git subtree for extern/dolphin keeps the imported SDK code connected to doldecomp/dolsdk2001 while still allowing local Melee-specific verification work.
The PR explicitly frames the import as work-in-progress instead of assuming SDK files match Melee unchanged, reducing risk of unverified library code being treated as canonical.
Separating external SDK sources from src/dolphin gives future contributors a clear migration target and makes eventual deletion of duplicated in-tree Dolphin code easier to track.
Calling out header include-path integration as manual work acknowledges convention mismatches early and avoids hiding build/header churn inside the import.
decomp_lessons: Do not blindly import external SDK source into a matching decomp project; each source file must be manually verified and tweaked against the target game's binary.
Keep external SDK code structurally separate from game-specific decomp code so replacements can be phased in gradually and audited.

Header compatibility is often a convention problem as much as a code problem; adding an external include directory may require manual adaptation to local naming, formatting, and declaration conventions.
A subtree can be a practical intermediate choice for shared decomp libraries because it preserves bidirectional contribution paths while avoiding immediate submodule workflow complexity.
naming_conventions: The imported Dolphin SDK area is named extern/dolphin.
External Dolphin headers are expected under extern/dolphin/include.
Existing in-tree Dolphin code is referred to as src/dolphin and is intended to be phased out over time.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Verify each external Dolphin source file manually against Melee before relying on it for matching.
Tweak imported SDK files as needed to match Melee rather than assuming dolsdk2001 sources are directly compatible.
Gradually replace src/dolphin files only after the corresponding extern/dolphin code is confirmed to work for Melee.
follow_up_queries: Which later PRs added extern/dolphin/include to Melee include paths?
Which src/dolphin files were later deleted or replaced by extern/dolphin equivalents?
Were any imported dolsdk2001 files modified specifically to match Melee?
Did extern/dolphin remain a git subtree or was it later converted to a submodule?
